title: TRANSDUCTION OF i3NEURONS (Support Protocol 4)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9a6f7","text":"Lentiviral infection of neurons can be used for a variety of applications in microscopy and biochemistry, provided that the transgene is driven by promoters not silenced in iPSCs or neurons (e.g., CAG, PGK In EF-1α, not CMV, see Critical Parameters and Troubleshooting). The precise parameters for optimal viral production and supernatant collection/concentration will depend on the type of virus used, viral packaging mechanism, viral packaging cell line, and specific transgene introduced. Highly efficient results have been observed with lentivirus produced by Lenti-X HEK cells, with transgenes carried in the pLEX backbone.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"616fp","text":"For each new viral preparation introduced to iPSCs, i3Neurons, or i3LMNs, titer should be assessed with a serial dilution of viral supernatant relative to the culture medium (E8, CM, or MM, respectively). This dilution typically begins with a 1:1 mixture of culture medium and viral supernatant, and reduces by 50 % in each subsequent well. Viral supernatant-containing medium should be removed from cells approximately 24 hr after addition, and replaced with fresh culture medium. With lentivirus, maximal transgene expression typically occurs 3 to 5 days after infection. Depending on the particular transgene, vector, and viral packaging system, initial expression can often be assayed 2 days after infection.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":53,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":67,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"1hum","text":"Please see SDS (Safety Data Sheet) for hazards and safety warnings.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Coat a 6-well dish with PLO (see Basic Protocol 6 ).

Check cells for confluency under an inverted microscope. Cells should be > 95 % confluent for maximal viral production efficiency If necessary, wait a day to transfect so that the cells are nearly confluent.
Plate 2.5 × 10 6 Lenti-X HEK cells in 6-well dish well in 1.5 mL DMEM/F12 containing 10 % FBS (HEK medium).
Warm two tubes with 150 µL Opti-MEM each to Room temperature .
Add 1.6 µg psPAX, 0.6 µg pMD302, 0.2 µg pAdvantage, 2.4 µg pLEX viral vector, and 10 µL P3000 reagent to tube A.
Add 1.87 µL Lipofectamine 3000 to tube B.
Incubate tubes at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Add the contents of tube A into tube B with a P200.
Incubate tube at Room temperature for 00:20:00 to 00:40:00 .
Add contents of tube dropwise to to the medium of Lenti-X cells with a P1000.
Agitate plate to evenly distribute transfection solution.
Replace medium on Lenti-X HEK cells with 3 mL of warm, fresh HEK medium supplemented with 6 µL of 500× ViralBoost reagent.
Check cells with a fluorescent microscope to ensure expression of any fluorescent proteins in the viral vector.
Check cells under inverted microscope for multinucleated morphology (indicating viral production) and fluorescent protein production.
Collect medium from well.

Optional: Replace medium on HEK cells with 1 mL to 2 mL fresh HEK medium, to be collected the following day. This process can be repeated until Day 6 to collect more virus. Be aware that viral titer decreases with each collection. Lentiviral preps can also be concentrated using various commercially available reagents (e.g., Clontech, cat. no. 631231 ).
Gently flick to mix.
Flick to mix.
Immediately flick the tube several times to mix.

Filter to purify virus from floating HEK cells. Instead of filtering, medium may be centrifuged at > for 00:10:00 at 4 °C to remove cells/debris, followed by transferring the supernatant to a new microcentrifuge tube.
Either use immediately or freeze aliquots at -80 °C .
creator: Julia Rossmanith
